title: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders
authors: Elisabeth M. Battinelli, Rebecca L. Zon

items.title: Understanding Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA) and its Clinical Implications
items.content: Thrombotic microangiopathy is a pathological condition characterized by endothelial cell injury in terminal arterioles and capillaries, leading to the formation of platelet and hyaline thrombi. This results in partial or complete occlusion of these vessels. TMA often presents with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA), which includes features like thrombocytopenia and schistocytes.
keyPoints: TMA involves endothelial cell injury leading to platelet and hyaline thrombi formation.

Commonly associated conditions include TTP, HUS, malignant hypertension, scleroderma renal crisis, antiphospholipid syndrome, preeclampsia/HELLP syndrome, HIV infection, cancer-associated TMA, and COVID-19-related microvascular disease.
TMA is often accompanied by MAHA with thrombocytopenia and schistocytes.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders | THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.title: Histopathological Features of Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA)
items.content: The histopathology of TMA includes swollen endocapillary cells, fibrin thrombi, platelet plugs, arterial intimal fibrosis, and a membranoproliferative pattern in the glomerulus. Fibrin thrombi may extend into the arteriolar vascular pole, causing glomerular collapse and potentially cortical necrosis.
keyPoints: Histopathological features of TMA include endotheliosis, fibrin thrombi, platelet plugs, arterial intimal fibrosis, and membranoproliferative pattern in the glomerulus.
Fibrin thrombi can extend into arteriolar vascular pole, leading to glomerular collapse and cortical necrosis.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders | THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.title: Distinguishing Features and Pathophysiology of HUS
items.content: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (HUS) is primarily characterized by MAHA and renal impairment, with STEC-HUS being the most common variant. It often follows hemorrhagic diarrhea in children under 5 years old but can affect adults as well. The pathophysiology involves Shiga toxins binding to Gb3 receptors on renal microvasculature cells, leading to inflammation and platelet aggregation.
keyPoints: HUS is defined by MAHA and renal impairment.
STEC-HUS (D⁺ HUS or EHEC HUS) is most common, often follows diarrhea in >80% of cases within 1 week.
Commonly affects children <5 years but can affect adults.
Shiga toxins bind to Gb3 receptors on renal cells, causing inflammation and platelet aggregation.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology and Clinical Presentation of aHUS
items.content: Atypical HUS (aHUS) results from complement dysregulation, often with low C3 and normal C4 levels. It can be congenital or acquired, frequently involving mutations in factor H or other complement-regulatory proteins. Renal injury is severe, potentially leading to end-stage renal disease.
keyPoints: aHUS involves complement dysregulation, typically with low C3 and normal C4.
Factor H deficiency is a common defect; over 70 mutations identified.
Renal injury can be severe, often resulting in end-stage renal disease.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology and Diagnostic Criteria for TTP
items.content: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP) is characterized by MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic symptoms, fever, and renal failure. It involves the accumulation of ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers due to decreased ADAMTS13 activity (<5–10%). An additional inflammatory trigger often initiates clinical TTP.
keyPoints: TTP is defined by MAHA with ADAMTS13 activity <5–10%.
Involves ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers due to low ADAMTS13.
<5% of patients present with the full pentad of symptoms.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.title: Incidence and Demographics of iTTP
items.content: iTTP is characterized by an autoantibody to ADAMTS13, leading to increased clearance or inhibition of its activity. The incidence rate in the U.S. is 2.9 cases per $10^6$ patients, with a median age of onset at 40 years. It occurs more frequently among blacks (more than nine times higher) and women (nearly three times higher compared to men).
keyPoints: Incidence rate: 2.9 cases/$10^{6}$ patients in the U.S.
Median age of onset: 40 years
Higher incidence among blacks (more than nine times)
Nearly three times higher incidence among women compared to men
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 2)

items.title: Drug-induced TMA and its Mechanisms
items.content: TMA can be induced by certain drugs, including chemotherapeutic agents like proteasome inhibitors and immunosuppressive agents such as cyclosporine. The mechanisms include toxic or endothelial damage (dose-dependent) and drug-induced autoantibodies (less dose-dependent). Quinine-associated TTP involves autoantibodies to various cells but not ADAMTS13, with a higher prevalence in women.
keyPoints: Chemotherapeutic agents: proteasome inhibitors, mitomycin C, gemcitabine
Immunosuppressive agents: cyclosporine, interferon, sirolimus, tacrolimus
Mechanisms: toxic/endothelial damage (dose-dependent) and drug-induced autoantibodies (less dose-dependent)
Quinine-associated TTP involves autoantibodies to granulocytes, lymphocytes, endothelial cells, platelet glycoprotein Ib/IX or IIb/IIIa complexes
Higher prevalence of quinine-associated TTP among women
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for aHUS and TTP: When to Use Plasma Exchange or Monoclonal Antibodies
items.content: In atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (aHUS), plasma exchange is effective due to its ability to replace complement-regulatory proteins. Anti-C5 monoclonal antibodies like eculizumab and ravulizumab are approved treatments that can halt Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA) and improve renal function. In contrast, TTP with combined factor H and ADAMTS13 deficiency is less responsive to plasma exchange, highlighting the need for tailored treatment approaches.
keyPoints: Plasma infusion/exchange replaces complement-regulatory proteins in aHUS.
Eculizumab and ravulizumab are effective anti-C5 monoclonal antibodies for aHUS.
Antibiotics and washed red cells are recommended for neuraminidase-associated HUS; avoid plasma/whole-blood transfusion due to IgM exacerbation of MAHA.
Drug-induced TMA typically does not respond to plasma exchange; C5 inhibitors may be used if refractory.
STEC HUS requires supportive measures as plasma exchange is ineffective.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.subtopic: HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY
items.title: Management of HSCT-TMA: Identifying Risk Factors and Treatment Approaches
items.content: HSCT-associated TMA occurs in ~8% of patients post-allogeneic HSCT, typically within the first 100 days. Key risk factors include conditioning regimens, immunosuppression, infections, graft-versus-host disease, female sex, and HLA-mismatched donor grafts. Diagnosis is challenging due to common post-HSCT symptoms like thrombocytopenia, anemia, and renal insufficiency. HSCT-TMA has a high mortality rate (75% within 3 months), but plasma exchange can be beneficial in patients with <25% ADAMTS13 activity.
keyPoints: HSCT-associated TMA develops post-allogeneic HSCT with an incidence of ~8%.
Occurs typically within the first 100 days of HSCT.
Risk factors include conditioning regimens, immunosuppression, infections, graft-versus-host disease, female sex, and HLA-mismatched donor grafts.
HSCT-TMA has a high mortality rate (75% within 3 months).
Plasma exchange is beneficial in patients with <25% ADAMTS13 activity.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.title: Management of Cancer-Associated Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA)
items.content: In cancer-associated TMA, the focus should be on treating the underlying malignancy rather than using plasma exchange or immunosuppression. This is because the mechanism involves tumor cell obstruction in microvasculature leading to platelet consumption and red blood cell fragmentation.
keyPoints: Cancer-associated TMA occurs with MAHA and thrombocytopenia, often presenting with bone pain and respiratory symptoms.
No role for plasma exchange or immunosuppression; treatment targets the underlying malignancy.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Scleroderma Renal Crisis
items.content: Scleroderma renal crisis, characterized by acute kidney injury and hypertension, occurs in 12% of diffuse systemic sclerosis patients. Diagnosis is supported by MAHA presence and histological findings similar to malignant hypertension.
keyPoints: Occurs in 12% of diffuse systemic sclerosis, 2% of limited systemic sclerosis.
Characterized by acute kidney injury, hypertension, MAHA, retinopathy, encephalopathy.
Histology shows arcuate artery intimal and medial proliferation with luminal narrowing.
ACE inhibitors reduce mortality from 90% at 1 month to 30% at 3 years.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.title: Therapeutic Goals in Scleroderma Renal Crisis
items.content: The primary treatment for scleroderma renal crisis is ACE inhibition, aiming to reduce blood pressure significantly within 24 hours. This therapy improves outcomes and renal recovery rates.
keyPoints: First-line therapy: ACE inhibitors; aim to reduce systolic BP by 20 mmHg and diastolic BP by 10 mmHg every 24 h.
ACE inhibition increases renal recovery rate post-dialysis initiation.
Kidney transplantation is not recommended for 2 years after starting dialysis.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.title: APLS and Stroke Risk: Why it's a Leading Cause in Young Patients
items.content: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APLS) is the leading cause of stroke in patients under 45 years old due to its association with systemic thrombosis, including arterial events. The presence of antiphospholipid antibodies such as lupus anticoagulant, anti-β2 glycoprotein I, and anticardiolipin increases the risk, especially when all three are positive (triple positive).
keyPoints: APLS is a leading cause of stroke in patients <45 years old.
Triple positivity for lupus anticoagulant, anti-β2 glycoprotein I, and anticardiolipin antibodies increases thrombosis risk.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID SYNDROME

items.title: Renal Involvement in APLS: Mechanisms and Treatment Strategies
items.content: The kidney is a primary site of vascular involvement in APLS, with arteriosclerosis affecting arcuate and intralobular arteries. Fibrous intimal hyperplasia and thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) are common findings. Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 2–3) is standard, while DOACs are generally not recommended for arterial events. Catastrophic APLS may require additional treatments like glucocorticoids, plasma exchange, IVIG, and possibly rituximab or eculizumab.
keyPoints: Renal involvement includes arteriosclerosis in arcuate and intralobular arteries.
Fibrous intimal hyperplasia and TMA are common renal biopsy findings.
Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 2–3) is standard; DOACs not recommended for arterial events.
Catastrophic APLS may require glucocorticoids, plasma exchange, IVIG, rituximab, or eculizumab.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID SYNDROME

items.title: Understanding HELLP Syndrome: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: HELLP syndrome is a severe pregnancy complication characterized by hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets. It often occurs in the third trimester but can present before week 28 or postpartum. While strongly associated with preeclampsia, up to 20% of cases occur without prior preeclampsia. Diagnosis is complicated due to similarities with MAHA, TTP, and aHUS, necessitating differentiation through specific markers such as ADAMTS13 activity levels and the lactate dehydrogenase to aspartate aminotransferase ratio.
keyPoints: Occurs in 0.2–0.9% of all pregnancies; 10–20% in severe preeclampsia
Mortality rate: 7.4–34%
Proteinuria present in 86–100% of cases
ADAMTS13 activity reduced by 30–60%, not to TTP levels (<10%)
LDH/AST ratio: 13:1 with preeclampsia, 29:1 without
Management includes magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and delivery based on symptom severity
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HELLP SYNDROME

items.title: Renal and Neurologic Complications in HELLP Syndrome
items.content: HELLP syndrome can lead to renal failure in about half of the patients, with findings indicative of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA). Neurological complications such as cerebral infarction and hemorrhage are life-threatening. The liver is a defining organ affected by HELLP, with risks including spontaneous rupture due to subcapsular hematomas. Nonfatal complications include placental abruption and Purtscher-like retinopathy.
keyPoints: Renal failure occurs in 50% of patients
Neurologic complications: cerebral infarction, hemorrhage, edema
Subcapsular hepatic hematomas can lead to liver rupture
Nonfatal complications: placental abruption, Purtscher-like retinopathy
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HELLP SYNDROME

items.title: Diagnosis and Renal Features of POEMS Syndrome
items.content: POEMS syndrome involves a constellation of symptoms including polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal gammopathy, and skin changes. Key diagnostic criteria include elevated VEGF levels, evidence of Castleman disease, or sclerotic bone lesions. Renal involvement often presents as unilateral kidney hypertrophy with glomerulomegaly and features reminiscent of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.
keyPoints: POEMS syndrome is characterized by polyneuropathy and monoclonal gammopathy, with >95% of the monoclonal light chain being lambda isotype.
Renal involvement includes unilateral kidney hypertrophy and glomerulomegaly.
Glomerular features include endothelial cell swelling, hypercellularity, mesangiolysis, microaneurysm, and enlargement.
Most patients present with mild to moderate renal impairment and low-grade proteinuria.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY

items.title: Renal Complications in Sickle Cell Disease
items.content: Sickle cell nephropathy results from vaso-occlusion in the renal medulla, leading to hemoglobin S polymerization and erythrocyte sickling. This causes hyposthenuria, hematuria, and papillary necrosis. The kidneys respond with increased blood flow and glomerular filtration rate due to prostaglandin mediation. Screening should include monitoring of blood pressure, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, and metabolic panel.
keyPoints: Renal complications are due to occlusion of the vasa recta in the renal medulla.
Proteinuria is present in 20–30% of patients, with nephrotic-range proteinuria linked to progression to renal failure.
Chronic kidney disease occurs in 25–33% of patients with sickle cell disease.
Hydroxyurea can decrease vaso-occlusive events and lessen albuminuria.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY

items.title: Identifying Renal Vein Thrombosis: Presentation and Etiologies
items.content: Renal vein thrombosis can present with symptoms such as flank pain, hematuria, rapid decline in renal function, and proteinuria, or it may be asymptomatic. It is often identified during investigations for pulmonary embolism. The left renal vein is more commonly affected, and two-thirds of cases are bilateral. Etiologies include endothelial damage (e.g., from homocystinuria, endovascular interventions), venous stasis (commonly due to dehydration in males or compression by retroperitoneal processes), and hypercoagulability (e.g., antiphospholipid syndrome, nephrotic syndrome).
keyPoints: Left renal vein more commonly involved.
Two-thirds of cases are bilateral.
Etiologies: endothelial damage, venous stasis, hypercoagulability.
Common causes: homocystinuria, dehydration (more common in males), retroperitoneal fibrosis, antiphospholipid syndrome, nephrotic syndrome.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · RENAL VEIN THROMBOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic and Treatment Approaches for Renal Vein Thrombosis
items.content: Doppler ultrasonography is a sensitive diagnostic tool for renal vein thrombosis, more so than standard ultrasonography. Computed tomography angiography offers near 100% sensitivity, while magnetic resonance angiography is an alternative despite higher costs. Treatment involves anticoagulation and addressing the underlying cause. In severe cases, endovascular thrombolysis may be considered, and nephrectomy might be necessary for life-threatening complications. For patients unable to receive anticoagulation, suprarenal inferior vena cava filters are an option.
keyPoints: Doppler ultrasonography is more sensitive than standard ultrasonography.
Computed tomography angiography has nearly 100% sensitivity.
Treatment includes anticoagulation and addressing underlying causes.
Endovascular thrombolysis for severe cases; nephrectomy for life-threatening complications.
Suprarenal inferior vena cava filters as an alternative when anticoagulation is not possible.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · RENAL VEIN THROMBOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA)?
options: Fibrin thrombi extending into arteriolar vascular pole
items.explanation: TMA is characterized by features such as swollen endocapillary cells, fibrin thrombi, and glomerular collapse; primary disorders of larger vessels are discussed elsewhere.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders | THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.question: Which condition is associated with thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) and involves hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count?
items.explanation: Preeclampsia/HELLP syndrome is associated with TMA and involves hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders | THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.question: What histopathological feature is integral to thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA)?
options: Platelet and hyaline thrombi causing occlusion
items.explanation: Platelet and hyaline thrombi causing partial or complete occlusion are integral to the histopathology of TMA.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders | THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical feature preceding MAHA in STEC HUS?
items.explanation: Diarrhea, often bloody, precedes MAHA within 1 week in >80% of cases.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.question: Which glycolipid surface receptor does Shiga toxin bind to in STEC HUS?
items.explanation: Shiga toxin binds to the glycolipid surface receptor globotriaosylceramide (Gb3).

items.question: What is the most common genetic defect associated with aHUS?
items.explanation: Factor H deficiency, the most common defect, has been linked to families with aHUS.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.question: What is required in addition to the absence of ADAMTS13 to often initiate clinical TTP?
items.explanation: Often, an additional inflammatory trigger (such as infection, surgery, pancreatitis, or pregnancy) is required to initiate clinical TTP.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of congenital thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (cTTP)?
items.explanation: cTTP is characterized by congenital deficiency of ADAMTS13.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) associated with chemotherapeutic agents like proteasome inhibitors?
items.explanation: The primary mechanism is toxic or endothelial damage.

items.question: Which drug-associated TTP is more common among women?
items.explanation: Quinine-associated TTP is more common among women.

items.explanation: Drug-induced TMA typically does not respond to plasma exchange and is treated primarily by discontinuing the use of the agent.

items.explanation: Plasma exchange is beneficial in HSCT-TMA when there is <25% ADAMTS13 activity.

items.explanation: Scleroderma renal crisis is characterized by arcuate artery intimal and medial proliferation with luminal narrowing.

items.explanation: The mortality rate has been lowered to 30% at 3 years with the use of ACE inhibitors.

items.question: Which antibodies are associated with antiphospholipid syndrome (APLS) and contribute to its classification criteria?
options: Anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies
Anti-nuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factor, anti-citrullinated protein antibodies
Anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies, ANCA, anti-dsDNA antibodies
Anti-smooth muscle antibodies, anti-mitochondrial antibodies, anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies
items.explanation: Antiphospholipid syndrome is characterized by antiphospholipid antibodies including anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, and anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies.

options: Warfarin, with an INR goal of 2–3

items.question: In which variant of APLS are thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) findings especially common?

items.question: Which laboratory marker ratio is typically higher in patients with HELLP syndrome and preeclampsia compared to those without preeclampsia?

Lactate dehydrogenase to aspartate aminotransferase ratio of 29:1
items.explanation: The lactate dehydrogenase to aspartate aminotransferase ratio is 13:1 in patients with HELLP syndrome and preeclampsia.

items.question: Which organ's complications are most definitive of HELLP syndrome?
items.explanation: The liver is the organ that defines HELLP syndrome, with complications such as subcapsular hepatic hematomas.

items.question: What percentage of patients with HELLP syndrome experience renal failure?
items.explanation: Renal failure occurs in half (50%) of the patients with HELLP syndrome.

items.question: Which of the following is a mandatory major criterion for diagnosing POEMS syndrome?
items.explanation: Polyneuropathy and monoclonal gammopathy are mandatory major criteria for POEMS syndrome.

items.explanation: Occlusion of the vasa recta in sickle cell disease can lead to papillary necrosis.

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of venous stasis leading to renal vein thrombosis in pediatric patients?

items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with nephrotic syndrome leading to renal vein thrombosis?

items.statement: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) is always present in thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA).
items.explanation: While TMA is usually accompanied by MAHA, it does not occur in all cases.

items.explanation: In kidneys recovering from acute TMA, secondary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis may develop.

items.explanation: ADAMTS13 deficiency is found in fewer than half of patients with clopidogrel-associated TTP.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 2)

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY
items.statement: Plasma and whole-blood transfusion should be avoided in neuraminidase-associated HUS.
items.explanation: These products contain IgM, which may exacerbate MAHA.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY
items.statement: HSCT-TMA typically occurs after 100 days of HSCT.
items.explanation: HSCT-TMA usually occurs within the first 100 days of HSCT.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.statement: Plasma exchange is a recommended treatment for cancer-associated thrombotic microangiopathy.
items.explanation: There is no role for plasma exchange in the management of cancer-associated TMA.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.statement: ACE inhibitors are recommended as first-line therapy for scleroderma renal crisis.
items.explanation: Treatment with ACE inhibition is the first-line therapy unless contraindicated.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.statement: APLS is the leading cause of stroke in patients over 45 years old.
items.explanation: APLS is the leading cause of stroke in patients under 45 years old.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID SYNDROME

items.statement: In APLS, DOACs are preferred over warfarin for arterial thrombotic events.
items.explanation: DOACs should never be used in those with arterial thrombotic events; warfarin is preferred.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID SYNDROME

items.statement: Proteinuria is present in less than 50% of HELLP syndrome cases.
items.explanation: Proteinuria is present in 86–100% of HELLP syndrome cases.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HELLP SYNDROME

items.statement: Plasma exchange has been shown to be beneficial in the treatment of HELLP syndrome.
items.explanation: Plasma exchange has no benefit in HELLP syndrome but may be indicated if TTP has not yet been ruled out.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HELLP SYNDROME

items.statement: Progression to end-stage renal disease is common in patients with POEMS syndrome.
items.explanation: Progression to end-stage renal disease is rare in POEMS syndrome.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY

items.statement: ACE inhibitors have been proven to prevent renal failure in sickle cell nephropathy.
items.explanation: While ACE inhibitors reduce proteinuria, data are lacking on their ability to prevent renal failure in sickle cell nephropathy.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY

items.statement: The left renal vein is less commonly involved in cases of renal vein thrombosis.
items.explanation: The left renal vein is more commonly involved in renal vein thrombosis.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · RENAL VEIN THROMBOSIS

items.statement: Endovascular thrombolysis may be considered for severe cases of renal vein thrombosis.
items.explanation: Endovascular thrombolysis is an option in severe cases of renal vein thrombosis.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · RENAL VEIN THROMBOSIS

items.assertion: TMA is characterized by endothelial cell injury in the terminal arterioles and capillaries.
items.reason: Platelet and hyaline thrombi causing partial or complete occlusion are integral to the histopathology of TMA.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders | THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.assertion: Factor H deficiency is the most common defect in aHUS.
items.reason: More than 70 mutations of the factor H gene have been identified, affecting its binding to C3b but not its concentration.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.assertion: ADAMTS13 activity below 5–10% is sufficient alone to produce TTP.
items.reason: The absence of ADAMTS13 alone may not by itself produce TTP; often, an additional inflammatory trigger is required.
items.explanation: A is true, but R is false.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME/THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY
items.assertion: HSCT-TMA carries a high mortality rate.
items.reason: The majority of patients have >10% ADAMTS13 activity, and plasma exchange is beneficial in <25%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the high mortality rate.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome/Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura | HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION-ASSOCIATED THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY

items.assertion: HIV-related thrombotic microangiopathy can be the first manifestation of HIV infection.
items.reason: It is seen in patients with advanced AIDS and low CD4+ T-cell counts.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it can occur even before advanced AIDS.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.assertion: Anticentromere antibody is associated with an increased risk of scleroderma renal crisis.
items.reason: It is a negative predictor for the disorder.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as anticentromere antibody is indeed a negative predictor.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · CANCER-ASSOCIATED TMA | PROGRESSIVE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (SCLERODERMA)

items.assertion: Hydroxyurea is used in the treatment of sickle cell disease to decrease vaso-occlusive events.
items.reason: Hydroxyurea has been shown to lessen albuminuria and reduce vaso-occlusive episodes in sickle cell patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hydroxyurea is used.
items.reference: Thrombotic Renovascular Disorders · POEMS SYNDROME | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY
